speaker: interviewer
text: Hedges. On the 20th of November, 1995. At what point did-- did things change for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: In which way? What did you mean by change?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you first become aware that-- when-- when were you first aware of fear?

speaker: interviewee
text: Of fear? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Well, we heard that the Germans approaching Hungary-- first of all, now that I think back, Romanians and Czechoslovakians came in a week crowd to Hungary, escaping to Hungary, and they told us the stories that they catching the Jews and taking them to Germany. That was probably a year or two before they came actually to Hungary.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you hearing that from?

speaker: interviewee
text: All this people who came to-- to escape from-- from the Germans, you know. It's not very hard to hear news when you are in a community where there's similarities, you know, all Jewish people coming and they-- they have, some have relatives. And they say to each other and you get to know everything soon. So we harbored them, as much as we could, I mean not personally me, but whoever could, and tried to keep them until the war is over, but they all thought that Hungary's left out and the war will finish before they get to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, it wasn't so, unfortunately, because when they came to us, no matter what nationality were you, that they caught up with you. And it was in 1943. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Practically the last of them, but still had enough time to take a lot of Hungarian Jews to concentration camps. They still existed. Maybe it was 1942. I don't think so, you know, '44 or-- '43, I think, they came to Hungary, 19th of March, I remember the date. 19th of March, 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Four.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember--

speaker: interviewee
text: And the war was finished in '50-- '45, so they-- they just barely have time to-- to kill a lot of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: What do you remember about the 19th of March, 1944?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very frightening experience, for sure, because then we realized that we are all in danger and there was no way to get out of it. We just were hoping that if we get to a ghetto, which they-- they-- they formed ghettos soon, and lots of people were sort of hiding. They're not hiding because everybody knew. They were closing the streets where the Jews were, and that's what we called the ghettos. And we thought that it's going to be soon finish and then we won't be going out of the country. But, one day came an order that every Jewish girl who is not married, or even if they're married, but they don't have little children, they have to get there in a certain point. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- I remember we were in the queue. We didn't take much with us, only what we had on, and we had to gather to go-- to go to a sports facility, which was open, only the-- the rows of seats, and we spend the night there. And then we were distributed for different working camps. We had to walk two or three days to-- to wherever we should go. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were you on your own?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, you think from the family? Yes. I was my-- on my own, but my very good friend from the building that I lived, she was with me all the way. We were together. She was younger than me, but she also follow that rule that she has to go. And that's how [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we both went and-- and started to dig trenches.

speaker: interviewer
text: The camp that you went to was two or three days walking from Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Budapest, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know-- do you know where it was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. It was two-- two different camps. One of them was [INAUDIBLE] and the other one was called [INAUDIBLE]. And whoever fit to one and that was full, then they take her to the other one. And we were in-- in not even in barracks, I don't remember. It was like just shelters. And we were 13, 14 months. And then, of course, the food was terrible. And we still could get some packages from home, whoever could afford it, and I remembered that-- and we had once a bottle of jam and we all started to eat it then, really we got sort of sick off it because we-- we started to eat so fast. It was starvation, probably.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was in 1943. Soon after they came in-- in and could be, in-- what months-- October, September October.

speaker: interviewer
text: The German occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But then it was, of course, before that. As soon as they came in and even before, the Hungarians caught a lot of Jewish men on the street, and they undressed them. That's how they find out whether they are Jewish, of course. And a lot of people were dragged out of their homes and taken to the Danube and they-- they just been shot. They said just walk in the water, and they were all shot and-- and left in the Danube. Dead. And children and the-- the-- you know. Women, children, no matter what.

speaker: interviewer
text: That was-- we were in a way maybe just lucky that we were not married and-- and-- and only those girls who taken away from all this horror. My poor sister who was in Hungary then, of course, and the first husband-- can I talk about that? The first husband, she didn't know when she married him that he was a Polish born, but living in Hungary, and speak proper Hungarian, that never knew, she never knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: But under the German law, she was considered Polish. And she already married the second husband when this happened, and because of the first husband, she was searched everywhere because they wanted to take her out of the country as-- as a non-Hungarian, and she was running every night to a different friend or-- or neighbor's place not to find her because she already had a chart.

speaker: interviewer
text: With the second husband?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the second husband who was taken away to-- to Russia.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Hungarian labor service?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So the-- then she had to-- to apply every week to this particular-- people who was in the office for-- for this purpose who was running away to the foreigners, let's call it, and she had to report every week. But then after she started to disappear, started to go places where they couldn't find her because she never got a permanent-- they didn't give her to live there permanently in Hungary. And, finally, I think that she had to pay somebody something, and they-- they allowed her to stay and that's when she settled down. Then, after, of course, she was looking for me.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this when you were-- had been taken away to the work camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. And I was taken away, and she was there with a child and no husband. And she had some little money left and she bought a Swiss passport for me. But by the time, it reached me. It would have reached each me, I was taken away to a hospital with dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were taken to hospital from the--

speaker: interviewee
text: From the working camp because I got that sickness which was contagious and, of course, they were afraid not only for the inmates, but that-- for themselves who was taking care of us. So they ship me to a hospital where there were only men, soldiers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where was the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's at-- It's called Daily [INAUDIBLE], it's-- it's very-- very close. It's-- it's the 13th precinct.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Budapest?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. They took me back to Budapest. And-- how do you call a hospital where there is only soldiers there?

speaker: interviewer
text: A military hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's right. [LAUGHS] Yes, I was taken there, and because I was the only woman logged into a room, a separate room, and they always [? unlocked ?] the room and they give me food. And they always looked after themselves so I couldn't escape or go anywhere. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] After a while, when my boss's wife found me--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where did she find you? In the hospital?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hospital. Yes. She was going from step to step until she found me that I am in the hospital and they let her in to visit me and she notified my husband. And, of course, my husband was also because-- she was a Jewish man, she was escaping in a soldier's uniform with-- with false papers.

speaker: interviewer
text: You weren't married yet, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, I wasn't married then.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Steven [? Heimann ?] then, but he already called himself [INAUDIBLE] because he was escaping with false papers. He was working-- as a matter of fact, this-- this school where I finished, the Jewish school, they made into a press for false papers. And he was working there, and he was distributing that. I'm sorry I didn't-- I have got a-- it's a funny thing I just remembered. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I have got [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] insert from my book, which-- from Israel, and, in that book, my husband does mention what a good deed he made with the Jewish people. And he sent me a copy of this page to see and show it to my family because he was like a hero. He was risking his life, and he went from place to place to distribute, wherever it was necessary, and he-- he could reach people. Then-- that's how I got my false papers and my sister too because he did it, and he did some for himself. And he came to visit me every day to this hospital. And you have to know that this time, when you enter this hospital, you had to show your papers. And he was always put himself into a risk that somebody discover that the papers are false. And he was-- went as far as went in to the-- the captain, the highest office in the hospital, and he said that I am in love with a Jewish girl who is-- who you are keeping in this hospital and I would like very much if you would let her go and just make an exception, because I want to marry her, no matter if she's Jewish or not. But, of course, they didn't let me out and then she-- he decided, my husband, still not my husband, he decided that he's giving me the papers, telling me the address where my sister was already with the boy, and then-- my sister just told me after I was talking to you-- I was talking to her, and she told me that the house where-- where she went to live with the false papers, it was a house where a lot of Jewish people lived and they were taken away. And you had to put in an application as a non-Jewish person whether you can occupy that flat. But she wasn't by herself there. There were a lot of others in the same flat. It was two or three bedrooms, and they couldn't afford to put everybody so they put a lot of them in the same flat.

speaker: interviewer
text: So my husband came in the hospital and he said that you only have one way to get out of here. If you take this paper with you, and open the window, when the-- the visitors from the hospital, that the-- the bell rings when the visiting was finished. When you hear the bell, you have to escape somehow. Because the door was locked, I couldn't go through the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: They wanted to take me back to-- to the military post from where the Germans taking the Jewish girls, who-- who-- you know, from-- they caught up with this labor camp. They couldn't keep-- they couldn't give them any more work, digging ditches or anything, so they-- they trying to shift everybody to Germany as-- as long as they have the power to do it. They didn't know then that they are defeated. And then, I get quite excited talking about that. So from the hospital--

speaker: interviewer
text: He told you you had to escape. How did you escape?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Well, I was lucky in a way because there was a-- a laundry. I was on the first floor. There was a laundry and there is a roof, a corrugated diamond roof on the top of the laundry, and then the ground floor. And I had to jump from the first floor to the top of the laundry and from then onto the ground floor. And I was lucky because it was on the back of the building and nobody noticed. And I was starting to-- to go faster to catch up with the crowd who was--

speaker: interviewer
text: What were you wearing?

speaker: interviewee
text: --leaving the hospital. I was wearing boots and high stockings, not stockings, it's-- it's-- you know, what you're wearing with going to-- to the mountains, the high-- the socks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Socks.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. And that-- I could only go out if I could shift myself between the crowd, and I must have been very lucky or very agile to do that. Not-- not even my papers were asked. I was just pushing myself out with the crowd, and I was rushing onto the first train, but was stopped there and I was getting on. Of course, I couldn't run because I didn't want to make a spectacle out of myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was just going steady and get on the first train. And then I went for a few stops and get off, and I went to the proper train that took me to this address where my sister was. And, of course, when she saw me and opened the door, she was-- we were crying both terribly. It was just a lucky escape. I think everybody who came out of this Holocaust is a lucky person. So the man the you married had provided you the papers and your sister the papers?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your sister had managed to find a place to stay? And in--

speaker: interviewee
text: He-- he found her the place as well, yes. And then, we-- we survived there, but they were bombing all the time, and we had to go down to the-- the celery. Cellar?

speaker: interviewer
text: Cellar.

speaker: interviewee
text: Cellar. Sorry. [LAUGHS] And because there were a lot of non-Jewish people as well in the building still, you know the-- the boy was-- her boy was about, oh, how old was-- was-- he? He was born in '40, just one second, '41. 1941, so he was just a two or three years old boy, and when he had to go to-- to do some [INAUDIBLE], we surrounded, you know, a few people who know the story, we surrounded him like it would be a game, so not to see that he is a Jewish boy. We had very, very hard time with that because a boy of this age is very hard to keep, not to-- to say what he shouldn't say.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there other people in the building then who knew that you were-- that you were Jews?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, fellow Jews, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there were other Jews in the building also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, because they all were with false papers. You know? But few of them, the original tenants, they were still there, and we didn't want them to-- to know what's happening. And, of course, it was very hard to get out because they were bombing all over. So my husband, who spoke Czechoslovakian and understood the Russian, oh, that was later on when the Russians came in.

speaker: interviewer
text: Wait, where-- where was your husband at this point?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was [INAUDIBLE]. You mean [INAUDIBLE] the Germans still? Yes. While-- while you were hiding? What was he doing? That-- he-- they had-- a few boys had a flat taken and this-- this was like underground work, what he done. You know? Trying to help people. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he come and visit you? Did you see him?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Every day and he was trying to get food to us. Whenever we could get some sugar which was very, very elementary, this time we need it to keep us alive or some potatoes or something that he could get-- laid hand on. He was very clever. He was very brave. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So, that was the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you feel?

speaker: interviewee
text: When?

speaker: interviewer
text: At this time. Were you frightened?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think I did. Funny, but I-- I think that-- it's coming with the age. That you think that it couldn't happen to you, anything bad because, as far as I can see, it's no use for-- like I say today to my grandchildren, that what happened then that they couldn't feel it or that they couldn't be afraid of-- of maybe something bad coming, going or it happens again, God forbid, or something like that. It's-- it's beyond them.

speaker: interviewer
text: So this time, when everything was going my way, I mean, in a way that I escaped, I thought that I have nothing to fear for. Because, first of all, this man is next to me, who was like a support, really, good to me and-- and to my family, and he was brave. And-- and I was just, felt secure with him. So I-- I wasn't really as far as I remember, I don't think I was really afraid.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was afraid in-- in the working camp. I was really, really afraid because I never been away from the family, and I was sort of teared away from them. It was very unnatural, and-- and I couldn't grasp it how it happened. But I think it was with everybody else the same. You know, and still. Still you're going back to stories and see them, and go into the pictures, and-- and torturing yourself, with all these sorts of stories which I-- I don't suppose I should any more. And my poor husband, of course, the whole relatives of-- of his-- his died in the-- he was by himself and the brother. And there were two sisters, his two sisters who had also died in the concentration camp. They were taken away from Slovakia and one had a-- a very beautiful little boy also with him-- with her and the husband. You know a lot of families died the way that there-- there were no one left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you and your sister and your nephew in-- in the-- in the-- in the flat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Until the-- until the-- they came in, the-- the Russians. No. Even then I was there. We were there until, yeah, the Russians came in, and we had to find a place, a proper place to live in. And we went back to the old place where we were originally.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Russians come in?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '44 January.

speaker: interviewer
text: '45. January '45. And how long do you think you'd been in the apartment with your sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: Over a year, I guess. Long time. I-- I really don't know it. I came back with-- no, no it wasn't a year, no, no. Six to eight months.

speaker: interviewer
text: So if we-- so if we work backwards, were the Germans-- the Germans came in in March and then you went to the work camp. From the work camp you went to the hospital and from the hospital you went to the-- to the apartment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. You're right.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a couple of months?

speaker: interviewee
text: Must have been more than that. Must have been more than that. I lost track of the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: It felt like a long time?

speaker: interviewee
text: It felt like a long time. Yes, really. Maybe it wasn't such a long time. For me, it was a very bad time because leaving these other people and-- and running all the time down in the cellar. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you go out ever into the streets at all during that time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I'll tell you what happened. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My boss was sort of not a religious Jew because he was married to a non-Jew. And they kept Christmas. And my husband, I have to call him my husband because later on he was my husband, and we went to see them where they lived for a Christmas celebration. I mean, they invited us for dinner. And when we went there and left after dinner, I was hit by a Russian part of the bomb. They bombed already. They were not properly in. They bombed from Buda to Pest. And we were on the street, about 10 o'clock at night, and we heard that-- that the bomb is coming. And he was in a leather jacket, and he was sort of covering me with the jacket, and hovering above me, so I don't get the-- the part of the bomb, if it hits us. And yet he was escaping, and I got here a very bad piece of shell. And, of course, it was gushing with blood, and-- and we had to run to the first aid place because there was no other-- there were a lot of first aid stations, and we went there and, of, course we had to explain it, that what we are doing out on the street and they gave me first aid help. And, you know, it was a very frightening experience because we were afraid even from the Russians. We didn't know.
